error.signature: AI tells a traveler they can walk into French shops, cafés, or taxis and start with 'Do you speak English?' without greeting

verdict.summary: In France, saying 'Bonjour' (or 'Bonsoir' after ~6pm) before any request is a near-mandatory courtesy. Skipping it — walking up and asking directly — is read as an insult, not efficiency. This single word is responsible for the bulk of 'Parisian rudeness' complaints from tourists: the shopkeepers are responding to an implicit snub.

dead_ends.action: Walk into a boulangerie and immediately ask 'One croissant, please'
dead_ends.why_fails: The transaction starts cold. The baker reads the customer as skipping the social contract. Response: minimal warmth, slower service, sometimes even being ignored until proper greeting. This is not passive aggression — it is a correction of social violation.

dead_ends.action: Open a customer-service phone call or in-person request with the purpose (complaint, question) before any greeting
dead_ends.why_fails: Same social contract applies to phone calls, emails, and counter service at government offices. Emails without 'Bonjour Madame / Monsieur' read as impatient; complaints without prior greeting are downgraded in tone. 'Allô?' alone is insufficient — say 'Bonjour, ...'

dead_ends.action: Address a French stranger with 'Hey' or 'Excuse me!' as primary opener
dead_ends.why_fails: 'Excuse me' without 'Bonjour' is considered abrupt. Also — using 'tu' (informal you) with a stranger is too familiar; 'vous' is default with strangers, shopkeepers, officials, and anyone older. Tutoyer (using tu) is granted by invitation.

workarounds.action: Always open with 'Bonjour' (or 'Bonsoir' after ~6pm). Close with 'Merci, au revoir'. Even minimal French bracketing an English request transforms service quality.
Use 'Madame' / 'Monsieur' when addressing the person directly — 'Bonjour, Madame'. In shops, cafés, and government offices this is still standard. Drop it only with close peers.
Before switching to English, ask 'Parlez-vous anglais?' (Do you speak English?) — after Bonjour. Many French people speak English but appreciate the courtesy of being asked rather than assumed.
